title: Lesson 406
transcript: Wouldn't it be cool if we had some sort of way of, especially in a large organization, keeping track of hundreds of thousands of different systems across the environment and making sure that none of those systems have vulnerabilities and that they're all configured correctly. Wouldn't it be cool if we had a way of somehow automating all of this? Because this is not an easy thing to do. So it'd be pretty awesome if we could somehow automate all of this. Enter SCAP, the Security Content Automation Protocol. This is exactly what SCAP is meant to do. So SCAP is a whole set of specifications, interoperable specifications that are meant to work together to help organizations automate their vulnerability management. and policy compliance, basically making sure that systems remain in compliance with policies, remain appropriately configured. So how does SCAP do this? Well, the way it does this is through a whole set of interoperable specifications. And I want to be clear here, you absolutely do not need to memorize all these names for the exam. The point here is, this is actually really complex. So SCAP is a whole initiative being driven by NIST, the National Institute of Standards Technologies, and a bunch of other really large organizations and government agencies in the US. They're all working together to try and create a bunch of specifications for how you could automatically maintain an inventory of all of your assets and then do automatic sort of assessment scans to make sure that those systems remain in compliance with their appropriate configuration to identify if those systems have vulnerabilities, to understand what the baseline configuration should be for a system, etc. So that's the idea of SCAP. It's a series of interoperable specifications that are meant to automate the whole vulnerability management process within an organization. which is subtly different from the next thing I'm gonna talk about, which is known as breach attack simulations. So here's what breach attack simulations is all about. Actually, let me take a step back here. Here was pretty common practice in a lot of organizations, not even that long ago. A lot of organizations would only do vulnerability scans like say once a quarter. I worked with a ton of organizations that would do this. And the reason they would do a vulnerability scan once a quarter is because that's what the PCI DSS standard mandated that they had to do. They had to do quarter vulnerability scans. So that's exactly what they would do. They would run a vulnerability scan every quarter. They'd find a whole bunch of stuff. They'd do a bunch of remediation work over a week or two. They might run one more scan just to show that those issues that were identified had been remediated. And then they wouldn't run another scan for another month or two. And is there a chance, a chance that a new vulnerability might be discovered in that intervening month or two between when they're doing scans? Of course. And that was the problem, right? it's probably not a good idea nowadays with all these different threats out there to just be doing a scan once a quarter. And so this is the whole idea behind breach attack simulation. The idea here is there are tools that are being developed. This is kind of cutting edge stuff, but there are tools now that will essentially automate the penetration testing and sort of automate the process of simulating attackers, of trying to continuously, constantly try and break into your organization to look to see where there's vulnerabilities so they can then be remediated. So breach attack simulation is basically automated the penetration testing process and doing it essentially continuously, constantly. So here's the subtle difference between SCAP and breach attack simulation. So as I said, right, SCAP is a whole extremely complicated suite of standards for automating vulnerability management and monitoring systems to make sure that they maintain compliance. Whereas breach attack simulation is a tool that you would buy. And that tool is just basically meant to be constantly scanning and trying to penetrate and test your systems to look for network vulnerabilities, system vulnerabilities, etc. So that's the subtle difference between these two. One final little bit of terminology I'll talk here about is compliance checks. Compliance checks are about reviewing the controls that an organization have in place and making sure they meet requirements. And there's a few different reasons why organizations will do this. Let's go back to PCI as an example. When would be a good time for an organization to take a look at their controls and decide, hey, if we're about to be PCI audited, do we have the right controls in place so that we're going to hopefully pass this PCI audit? When's the right time to be doing that? A day before the QSA shows up to audit? No. The ideal time that an organization should be checking to see, hey, do we have the right controls in place so that when we get PCI audited, we're not going to have a whole bunch of major deficiencies. When that check should be done is probably 18 to 24 months before the QSA shows up. And this is where you can use compliance checks. If you have to, if an organization has to be PCI compliant, the organization can say, OK, well, here's the PCI controls, all thousand PCI controls. And now let's look at the controls that we have in place operating within our organization. And you can see, are there any gaps? Are there any controls that, you know, they might either there's no control or there's a control in place, but it doesn't provide sufficient assurance or we do have everything we need. a compliance check is where you're looking for this. And you obviously, like in the case of PCI, where you're going to be audited, you want to do this compliance check probably 18 to 24 months ahead of when the PCI auditor shows up. Because that way, any deficiencies that are identified, you've got a year to six months to remediate those deficiencies, get controls in place, and then you can make sure those controls are operating effectively for a year. Because when the QSA shows up, they're often going to look back on the controls that have been operating over the previous year or so. This is a perfect example of when you'd want to do compliance checks. If you have a compliance requirement, check that you're going to be making sure that you're in compliance with whatever that contractual regulatory framework is before the auditor shows up. Another really good use of compliance checks is just to see if an organization is in compliance with industry best practices. So an organization might have sort of organically grown their own security controls, developed their own security controls. And the organization might say, like, hey, I wonder how we align to sort of an industry best practice, like, say, ISO 27001. So this is where you can do a compliance check as well. You can basically, and this often is always well referred to as a gap assessment. You'll take ISO 27001, you'll take the controls that the organization has in place, and you just look to see if there's any gaps. It's very similar to a compliance check, same sort of idea. So there you go. That's compliance checks.
